title: Foreign Policy and International Relations of the Republic of Kazakhstan
overview: Lecture 10 covers Kazakhstan's foreign policy over 30+ years of independence: renunciation of the world's 4th-largest nuclear arsenal, accession to the NPT and CTBT, the UN-designated International Day Against Nuclear Tests; promotion of Eurasianism and the creation of the EAEU (2015); the CICA initiative (1992), the G-Global platform (2012), the EXPO-2017 'Future Energy'; chairmanship of the OSCE (2010) with the Astana Summit; participation in the UN (non-permanent member of the Security Council 2017–2018), the SCO (founding member 2001), the EurAsEC and EAEU; balanced multi-vector diplomatic relations with the USA, China, Russia, Türkiye, and the EU; and the Concept of Foreign Policy of the Republic of Kazakhstan for 2020–2030.

coreConcepts.definition: Core principle: balanced, pragmatic and proactive relations with all key centres of power simultaneously.
coreConcepts.example: Strategic partnerships with Russia, China, the USA, the EU and Türkiye in parallel.

coreConcepts.definition: Renunciation of the 4th-largest nuclear arsenal in the world inherited from the USSR; accession to the NPT (1992) as a non-nuclear state; transfer of warheads to Russia completed in 1994; signing of the CTBT in 1996.
coreConcepts.example: Closure of the Semipalatinsk Test Site (29 August 1991); UN resolution 2009 — 29 August declared International Day of Action Against Nuclear Tests on Kazakhstan's initiative.

coreConcepts.definition: Conference on Interaction and Confidence Building Measures in Asia — proposed by Nazarbayev at the 47th UN General Assembly in 1992.
coreConcepts.example: First summit — Almaty 2002. 6th summit — Astana 2022 under Tokayev's chairmanship; 27 participating states (more than half of the world's population).

coreConcepts.definition: Eurasian Economic Union — launched 1 January 2015; members: Armenia, Belarus, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Russia.
coreConcepts.example: Idea first proposed by Nazarbayev at Moscow State University on 29 March 1994; treaty signed in 2014.

coreConcepts.definition: Kazakhstan was the first Central Asian and post-Soviet state to chair the OSCE.
coreConcepts.example: OSCE Astana Summit, December 2010 — first OSCE summit in 11 years; 56 participating states.

coreConcepts.term: Congress of Leaders of World and Traditional Religions
coreConcepts.definition: Inter-religious dialogue platform held in Astana since 2003.
International dialogue platform proposed by Nazarbayev in 2012, based on multilateralism, transparency and equal participation.
coreConcepts.example: Astana Economic Forum is held annually under the G-Global framework.

coreConcepts.definition: International specialized exhibition hosted by Astana — theme 'Future Energy'.
coreConcepts.example: First EXPO in Central Asia; focus on renewable energy and sustainable development.

coreConcepts.definition: Document approved in March 2020 defining the principles and strategic goals of Kazakh foreign policy for the decade.
coreConcepts.example: Principles: stable, fair, democratic world order; equal integration into the global space; protection of citizens abroad; multi-vector, pragmatic, proactive diplomacy.

expandedNotes.heading: Initiatives of Kazakhstan in Foreign Policy
expandedNotes.body: Over 30 years of independence Kazakhstan has become an influential member of the world community. NUCLEAR DISARMAMENT was one of the first and most significant initiatives: in 1991 Kazakhstan inherited the 4th-largest nuclear arsenal in the world, but made a strategic decision for a nuclear-free future. In 1992 — accession to the NPT; in 1994 — completion of the transfer of warheads to Russia; in 1996 — signature of the CTBT; in 2009 the UN General Assembly proclaimed 29 August the International Day of Action Against Nuclear Tests on Kazakhstan's initiative. EURASIANISM: Nazarbayev proposed the concept of a Eurasian Union; in 2014 the treaty establishing the EAEU was signed (Armenia, Belarus, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Russia). INTERRELIGIOUS DIALOGUE: the Congress of Leaders of World and Traditional Religions in Astana since 2003.

expandedNotes.heading: CICA and G-Global
expandedNotes.body: Kazakhstan actively advocates the creation of international dialogue platforms. CICA — Conference on Interaction and Confidence Building Measures in Asia — was proposed by Nazarbayev at the 47th UN General Assembly in 1992 to strengthen peace, security and stability in Asia through confidence-building measures and cooperation. Today CICA unites 27 participating states. G-Global is another Kazakhstan initiative (2012) — an international dialogue platform discussing global problems on principles of multilateralism, transparency and equal participation; the annual Astana Economic Forum is one of its key events. EXPO-2017 in Astana — first EXPO exhibition in Central Asia, devoted to 'Future Energy' — brought global attention to renewable energy and sustainable development.

expandedNotes.heading: Participation in International Organizations
expandedNotes.body: Since 1991 Kazakhstan has been an active participant in numerous international organizations: OSCE, UN, SCO, EurAsEC/EAEU. In 2010 Kazakhstan became the first Central Asian country to chair the OSCE, organising the Astana Summit (56 participating states) — proposed a new concept of security combining political-military, humanitarian, economic and environmental dimensions. 2017–2018 — Kazakhstan was a NON-PERMANENT MEMBER OF THE UN SECURITY COUNCIL, focused on nuclear disarmament, counter-terrorism, climate change, green economy and global food security. In 2023 — 30th anniversary of UN membership; Tokayev addressed the General Assembly raising five key issues: (1) nuclear disarmament and non-proliferation, (2) combating climate change and green technologies, (3) global food security, (4) fight against terrorism and extremism, (5) sustainable socio-economic development. SCO — founding member (2001), strengthening trust between Central Asia and China, regional security, transport-logistics-energy projects. EAEU — economic integration to create a single space, eliminate trade barriers, coordinate economic policies.

expandedNotes.heading: Diplomatic Relations with Leading Countries
expandedNotes.body: Established diplomatic relations with 186 countries and signed 4,163 international treaties. USA: cooperation in economics, security and education; energy is a key area — major US investors in Kazakh oil & gas (Chevron, ExxonMobil); 2006 — 'Charter on Strategic Partnership'; joint fight against terrorism, extremism and drug trafficking. CHINA: strategic partnership; one of Kazakhstan's largest trading partners; cooperation under the 'Belt and Road Initiative'; 2013 — agreement on good neighbourliness, friendship and cooperation; investment in infrastructure, transport, energy. RUSSIA: key strategic partner; close historical ties; EAEU and CSTO; Baikonur Cosmodrome; coordination in international forums. TÜRKİYE: was one of the first countries to recognize Kazakhstan's independence (16 December 1991); Organization of Turkic States; investment in construction, energy, education. EUROPE / EU: 2015 — Enhanced Partnership and Cooperation Agreement covering trade, investment, energy, transport and human rights; key partner of the EU in Central Asia; cooperation in renewable energy and diversification of supplies.

expandedNotes.heading: Concept of Foreign Policy 2020–2030
expandedNotes.body: Approved in March 2020. BASIC PRINCIPLES: (1) building a stable, fair and democratic world order; (2) equal integration into the global political, economic and humanitarian space; (3) effective protection of the rights, freedoms and legitimate interests of Kazakhstanis and compatriots abroad; (4) promoting external openness and favourable external conditions to raise well-being; (5) multi-vector, pragmatism and proactivity — friendly, equal and mutually beneficial relations with all states and international organisations of practical interest. STRATEGIC GOALS: strengthening independence, state sovereignty and territorial integrity; maintaining the leading position and long-term interests of Kazakhstan in Central Asia; positioning the country as an active and responsible participant in the international community; maintaining friendly, predictable and mutually beneficial relations bilaterally and multilaterally; full use of foreign-policy potential to increase the competitiveness of the national economy and the quality of life of Kazakhstanis.

keyScholars.name: Nursultan Nazarbayev
keyScholars.contribution: First President of Kazakhstan; architect of the multi-vector doctrine; initiator of the renunciation of nuclear weapons, CICA (1992), the Eurasian Union (1994 idea → EAEU 2015), G-Global (2012) and the Congress of Leaders of World and Traditional Religions (2003).

keyScholars.name: Kassym-Jomart Tokayev
keyScholars.contribution: Career diplomat; Minister of Foreign Affairs of Kazakhstan (1994–1999, 2002–2007); Director-General of the UN Office at Geneva (2011–2013); President of Kazakhstan from 2019 — chair of the 6th CICA Summit (2022); approved the Concept of Foreign Policy 2020–2030.

comparativeTables.title: Key Foreign Policy Initiatives of Kazakhstan
rows: Accession to NPT; CICA initiative at UN
Kazakhstan recognized as a non-nuclear state
Transfer of warheads completed; Eurasian Union idea
End of nuclear testing legally fixed
1st Congress of Leaders of World and Traditional Religions
Common economic space with Russia, Belarus, Armenia, Kyrgyzstan
Active role in global security agenda

realWorldExamples: 29 August — International Day Against Nuclear Tests (UN, on Kazakhstan's initiative, 2009).
Diplomatic relations with 186 countries; over 4,163 international treaties signed.
Astana Economic Forum — annual G-Global event.
Baikonur Cosmodrome — Russia's main space-launch site, leased from Kazakhstan.
6th CICA Summit (2022) — Tokayev welcomed Kuwait as a new participant; revised the Confidence Building Measures Catalogue (epidemics, healthcare, ICT security).
Kazakhstan is a key transit hub in the Trans-Caspian International Transport Route.

timeline.event: Türkiye — first country to recognize Kazakhstan's independence
Accession to the NPT; CICA initiative at 47th UN General Assembly
Transfer of nuclear warheads to Russia completed; Eurasian Union proposed at MSU
Signature of the Comprehensive Nuclear Test Ban Treaty (CTBT)
Kazakhstan becomes a founding member of the SCO
First Congress of Leaders of World and Traditional Religions
Charter on Strategic Partnership with the USA
UN proclaims 29 August International Day Against Nuclear Tests
Good Neighbourliness, Friendship and Cooperation Treaty with China
Launch of the EAEU; Enhanced Partnership Agreement with the EU
Non-permanent member of the UN Security Council
6th CICA Summit in Astana under Tokayev's chairmanship
30th anniversary of Kazakhstan's UN membership; Tokayev addresses the UN General Assembly

mustMemorize: Multi-vector policy = pillar of Kazakhstan's foreign policy
Türkiye — first to recognize independence (16 Dec 1991)
1992 — accession to the NPT as a non-nuclear state
1994 — transfer of warheads to Russia completed (Lisbon Protocol)
29 August — International Day Against Nuclear Tests (UN, 2009)
CICA initiative — Nazarbayev, 47th UNGA, 1992; 1st summit Almaty 2002
Concept of Foreign Policy 2020–2030 — approved March 2020
cheatSheet:
Türkiye (1991) → UN + NPT + CICA (1992) → CTBT (1996) → SCO (2001) → CICA summit + Religions (2002–2003) → 29 Aug Day (2009) → OSCE chairmanship (2010) → G-Global (2012) → EAEU (2015) → EXPO (2017) → UNSC (2017–18) → Foreign Policy Concept (2020) → CICA 6 (2022)
Five vectors: Russia, China, USA, EU, Türkiye
Three platforms: CICA, G-Global, Congress of Religions
mnemonics: OSCE-2010 = '20-10 in Astana' (first summit in 11 years)
5 vectors = Russia / China / USA / EU / Türkiye
3-2-1 platforms: 3 — Congress of Religions (2003); 2 — CICA (2002); 1 — G-Global (2012, single Kazakh idea for the world)
